summary: Joel Spolsky describes his three-part phone screening process for programmer candidates, designed to efficiently filter out poor fits before costly in-person interviews. The first part explores career history to assess problem-solving ability and passion, drilling into both technical depth and how candidates navigated organizational challenges. The second part poses an open-ended design question to evaluate algorithmic thinking through conversation rather than rote coding. The third part lets candidates ask questions, which doubles as a test of preparation and genuine interest. Spolsky argues phone screens are not only cheaper but fairer than in-person interviews, since they reduce snap judgments based on appearance. Even with this filter, only about one in three candidates who pass the phone screen ultimately get hired.
key_points: Phone screens eliminate about half of candidates who look great on paper, saving significant time and money before in-person interviews.
Phone interviews reduce appearance-based bias by forcing interviewers to evaluate what candidates actually say rather than how they look.
Drilling into technical details and project roles uncovers exaggeration, while trick questions test whether candidates have genuine affinity for technical truth.
Open-ended design questions are superior to rote coding questions for phone screens because they enable natural conversation about algorithms, tradeoffs, and problem-solving approaches.
The interview is bidirectional: smart candidates have choices, so the process must also sell them on the company.
money_quotes: I've had people with great resumes tell me a pointer should fit in one byte.
With a phone interview, because you can't see the person, it's easier to focus on the quality of what they're saying rather than other external factors not relevant to their job.
Smart programmers have a certain affinity for the truth, and they'll call you on it.

The bottom line in my interviewing technique is that smart people can generally tell if they're talking to other smart people by having a conversation with them on a difficult or highly technical subject.
The interview question is really just a pretext to have a conversation on a difficult subject so that the interviewer's judgment can form an opinion on whether this is a smart person or not.
If you find yourself explaining everything three times, either you're terrible at explaining things, or you're talking to someone who is not that smart.
Failing to do even the most basic homework before the interview does not give me a great deal of confidence in the candidate's ability to be smart or to get things done.
The interview process is as much a way for the candidate to decide if they want to work for us as it is a way for us to decide if we want to hire the candidate.
key_insight: Phone screens are both a cost-saving filter and a fairness mechanism that forces interviewers to evaluate candidates on substance rather than appearance, using open-ended technical conversations as a proxy for general intelligence and problem-solving ability.
